Wheat stands ready for harvest at a farm in Eugowra, Australia, November 19, 2025. REUTERS/Hollie Adams/File Photo [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/wheat-stands-ready-for-harvest-at-a-farm-in-eugowra-australia/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjU6bmV3c21sX1JDMkdaSEFNSFVMVA%3D%3D/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

May 2 (Reuters) - Australia's government said on Saturday it was worried about a plague of mice ​in the west and would keep ‌working with industry to curb its impact on food supply.

"Obviously we're concerned about the mice situation ​including in Western Australia," Energy Minister ​Chris Bowen said in televised remarks ⁠from Sydney.

The Reuters Iran Briefing newsletter keeps you informed with the latest developments and analysis of the Iran war. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-iran-briefing/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fworld%2Fasia-pacific%2Faustralia-worried-over-mouse-infestations-impact-food-supply-2026-05-02%2F)

According to local media, mice - a [persistent ​problem](https://www.reuters.com/business/environment/australias-mice-plague-overwhelms-farmer-new-south-wales-2021-05-26/) in Australian grain-growing regions - have been ​infesting farms in the wheatbelt state of Western Australia. The situation prompted grain producers in April to ​warn of crop losses and call ​for access to more powerful mice poison.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Bowen said the ‌centre-left ⁠government was continuing to work hard with industry to reduce the plague's impact on local and overseas food supply.

"A mice plague ​is a ​very difficult ⁠situation for farmers and for industry and for governments," he added.

Australia, ​the world's fourth-largest [wheat exporter,](https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/australian-growers-shift-less-fertiliser-intensive-crops-iran-war-costs-surge-2026-04-02/) suffered ​its ⁠worst mouse plague in 1993, when the rodents destroyed thousands of hectares of crops and ⁠attacked ​livestock in pig and ​chicken farms, according to the country's science agency.

Reporting by ​Sam McKeith in Sydney; Editing by William Mallard

May 2 (Reuters) - Australia’s government said on Saturday it was worried about a plague of mice in the west and would keep working with industry to curb its impact on food supply.

“Obviously we’re concerned about the mice situation including in Western Australia,” Energy Minister Chris Bowen said in televised remarks from Sydney.

According to local media, mice - a persistent problem in Australian grain-growing regions - have been infesting farms in the wheatbelt state of Western Australia. The situation prompted grain producers in April to warn of crop losses and call for access to more powerful mice poison.

Bowen said the centre-left government was continuing to work hard with industry to reduce the plague’s impact on local and overseas food supply.

“A mice plague is a very difficult situation for farmers and for industry and for governments,” he added.

Australia, the world’s fourth-largest wheat exporter, suffered its worst mouse plague in 1993, when the rodents destroyed thousands of hectares of crops and attacked livestock in pig and chicken farms, according to the country’s science agency.

(Reporting by Sam McKeith in Sydney; Editing by William Mallard)

2 maggio (Reuters) - Il governo australiano ha dichiarato sabato di essere preoccupato per una piaga di topi nell’ovest e che continuerà a lavorare con l’industria per limitare il suo impatto sull’approvvigionamento alimentare.

“Ovviamente siamo preoccupati per la situazione dei topi, anche nell’Australia occidentale”, ha detto il ministro dell’Energia Chris Bowen in un intervento televisivo da Sydney.

Secondo i media locali, i topi - un problema persistente nelle regioni australiane produttrici di grano - hanno infestato le aziende agricole nello Stato della cintura di grano dell’Australia occidentale. La situazione ha spinto i produttori di grano ad aprile a lanciare avvertimenti sulle perdite di raccolto e a chiedere l’accesso a un veleno per topi più potente.

Bowen ha dichiarato che il governo di centro-sinistra sta continuando a lavorare duramente con l’industria per ridurre l’impatto della piaga sulle forniture alimentari locali e d’oltreoceano.

“La piaga dei topi è una situazione molto difficile per gli agricoltori, per l’industria e per i governi”, ha aggiunto.

L’Australia, quarto esportatore di grano al mondo, ha subito la peggiore epidemia di peste dei topi nel 1993, quando i roditori hanno distrutto migliaia di ettari di colture e attaccato il bestiame negli allevamenti di suini e polli, secondo l’agenzia scientifica del Paese.

(Reporter: Sam McKeith a Sydney; editor: William Mallard)

# Australia worried over impact of mouse infestation on food supply

[Sign up now:](https://www.straitstimes.com/newsletter-signup?ref=inline-article#nl_asian_insider) Get insights on Asia's fast-moving developments

![FILE PHOTO: Wheat stands ready for harvest at a farm in Eugowra, Australia, November 19, 2025. REUTERS/Hollie Adams/File Photo](https://cassette.sphdigital.com.sg/image/straitstimes/a76b52f7ec44b836576052bd18503ff56dd79d57d6d966cd5f2f58a94ca38c25)

Mice are a persistent problem in Australia's grain-growing regions.

PHOTO: REUTERS

[![Google Preferred Source badge](https://www.straitstimes.com/assets/google-preferred-source-badge-RDgZuryM.png)](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=https://www.straitstimes.com)

PublishedMay 02, 2026, 10:13 AM

UpdatedMay 02, 2026, 01:10 PM

Listen

Australia’s government said on May 2 that it was worried about a plague of mice in the west of the country and would keep working with industry to curb its impact on food supply.

“Obviously we’re concerned about the mice situation including in Western Australia,” Energy Minister Chris Bowen said in televised remarks from Sydney.

According to local media, mice – a persistent problem in Australia’s grain-growing regions – have been infesting farms in the wheatbelt state of Western Australia.

The situation prompted grain producers in April to warn of crop losses and call for access to more powerful mice poison.

Mr Bowen said the centre-left government was continuing to work hard with industry to reduce the plague’s impact on local and overseas food supply.

“A mice plague is a very difficult situation for farmers and for industry and for governments,” he added.

Australia, the world’s fourth-largest wheat exporter, suffered its worst mouse plague in 1993, when the rodents destroyed thousands of hectares of crops and attacked livestock in pig and chicken farms, according to the country’s science agency. REUTERS
